coredata.dc:title: Rapid and sensitive detection of methyl parathion in rice based on carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescence probe and inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruiting
Zhang, Liyuan
Yu, Runzhong
Wang, Changyuan
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive fluorescent sensing system of novel carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe based on corn stalks was established for the determination of methyl parathion by alkaline catalytic hydrolysis and inner filter effect mechanism. The carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe was prepared from corn stalks using an optimized one-step hydrothermal method. The detection mechanism of methyl parathion was revealed. The reaction conditions were optimized. The linear range, sensitivity and selectivity of the method were evaluated. Under the optimal conditions, the carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity to methyl parathion, achieving a linear range of 0.005–14 µg/mL. The fluorescence sensing platform was applied to the detection of methyl parathion in rice samples, and the results showed that the recoveries range from 91.64 to 104.28 %, and the relative standard deviations were less than 4.17 %. The detection limit for methyl parathion in rice samples was 1.22 µg/kg, and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 4.07 µg/kg, which was very satisfactory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methyl parathion
Carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescence probe
Inner filter effect
Sensor
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescence probe
Characterization of carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe
Characterization of carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe
Linear range and limit of detection (LOD)
Detection of MP in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Central Government for the Reform and Development of Local Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant number: 2020YQ16), the key research and development program guidance project of Heilongjiang province (Grant number: GZ20210071), the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant number: 2018YFE0206300).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of CQDs-NFP and the detection mechanism of MP.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-NP (hydrolyzed by MP) and fluorescence spectrum of CQDs-NFP (B) Fluorescence decay curve of CQDs-NFP solution with and without MP.
(A) Fluorescence responses of CQDs-NFP and MP at different pH (B) Absorption spectra of MP at different hydrolysis degrees.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-NFP after adding different concentrations of MP (B) Fitted scatter plot of different MP concentrations and the change in fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of CQDs-NFP (C) UV–vis spectra of CQDs-NFP with different MP concentrations.
Spike determination of MP in rice samples with CQDs-NFP sensor.
Comparison of IFE-based fluorescence sensor with other fluorescence sensors for detecting MP.

head.ce:title: Rapid and sensitive detection of methyl parathion in rice based on carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescence probe and inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Heilongjiang Bayi Agricultural University, 5 Xinfeng Road, Daqing 163319, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Heilongjiang Bayi Agricultural University, 5 Xinfeng Road, Daqing 163319, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information and Electrical Engineering, Heilongjiang Bayi Agricultural University, 5 Xinfeng Road, Daqing 163319, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Information and Electrical Engineering, Heilongjiang Bayi Agricultural University, 5 Xinfeng Road, Daqing 163319, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Agro-products Processing and Quality Safety of Heilongjiang Province, Daqing 163319, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Agro-products Processing and Quality Safety of Heilongjiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Agro-products Processing and Quality Safety of Heilongjiang Province, Daqing 163319, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive fluorescent sensing system of novel carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe based on corn stalks was established for the determination of methyl parathion by alkaline catalytic hydrolysis and inner filter effect mechanism. The carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe was prepared from corn stalks using an optimized one-step hydrothermal method. The detection mechanism of methyl parathion was revealed. The reaction conditions were optimized. The linear range, sensitivity and selectivity of the method were evaluated. Under the optimal conditions, the carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity to methyl parathion, achieving a linear range of 0.005–14 µg/mL. The fluorescence sensing platform was applied to the detection of methyl parathion in rice samples, and the results showed that the recoveries range from 91.64 to 104.28 %, and the relative standard deviations were less than 4.17 %. The detection limit for methyl parathion in rice samples was 1.22 µg/kg, and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 4.07 µg/kg, which was very satisfactory.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methyl parathion (MP) has been widely applied in agricultural production because of its high efficacy, broad-spectrum characteristics, and toxicity to insects via contact and ingestion routes. It is often processed into an emulsified concentrate or powder and primarily used to reduce a variety of pests in rice, fruits, vegetables, and other crops to decline yield losses (Muckoya, Nomngongo, & Ngila, 2020). The widespread use of MP has resulted in residues in soil, air, water, and crops (He et al., 2021). The Chinese national regulation stipulates that the maximum residue level of MP in rice is 0.02 mg/kg (National food safety standard-determination of 208 pesticides and metabolites residues in foods of plant origin-gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry method: GB 23200.113-2018, 2018). MP enters organisms through various routes, including the skin, mucosa, eyes, respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts, it becomes toxic to humans and animals by inhibiting synaptic acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which results in the accumulation of acetylcholine (ACh), excessive stimulation of its receptors in synapses, and eventual lead to serious disruption to nervous system, the health effects can be acute or chronic (Fukuto, 1990). Therefore, it is important to design and establish a rapid and effective analytical method for the detection of MP residues in food.

Various methods have been developed for the detection of MP, including gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy. Although these methods can achieve highly sensitivity, selectivity, and quantitativity detection of MP, they have unavoidable disadvantages, such as high cost of the instruments, complex and time-consuming pre-treatment, and the need for skilled operators limit their application in real-time fields. A variety of electrochemical (Cui et al., 2018) and optical sensors (Liu et al., 2021) for MP detection have been developed, including a combined molecularly imprinted polymer electrochemical sensor that detects MP phosphorus. However, the preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers requires a large number of template molecules and repeated elution, resulting in an expensive and cumbersome preparation process. In addition, the preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers cannot be commercialized, hence, the practical scope of this detection method is limited (Esen, Czulak, Cowen, Piletska, & Piletsky, 2018). Optical sensors include fluorescence sensors, most of which involve enzyme-catalyzed and inhibitory reactions with acetylcholinesterase (AChE) (Yang et al., 2021) and organophosphorus hydrolase (OPH) (Mehta et al., 2019). However, the enzyme activity might be inhibited by the different conditions, and the detection conditions are difficult to control, which hinders the development of this technique. Fluorescence sensing has the advantages of high sensitivity and accuracy, therefore, it is practical significance to develop non-enzymatic fluorescence sensors to detect MP. Carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probes (CQDs-NFP) are novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes of less than 10 nm (Chen et al., 2022). The particles of CQDs-NFP are generally uniform in size and exhibit excitation wavelength (LEX) dependent photoluminescence (PL) behaviour. They have the advantages of low manufacturing cost, good water solubility, low toxicity, unique optical properties, and good biocompatibility. CQDs-NFP can be prepared from a wide variety of raw materials in addition to common reagents. They have been prepared from various biological wastes, such as kombucha tea (Baslak, Demirel, Kocyigit, Alatli, & Yildirim, 2022), broccoli (Elsherbiny, Shao, Acheampong, Khalifa, Liu, & Huang, 2022), jasmine leaves (Kumari, & Sahu, 2022), gulfweed (Castañeda-Serna, Calderón-Domínguez, García-Bórquez, Salgado-Cruz, & Farrera, 2022)and sludge (Chen et al., 2022). China is a large agricultural country with widespread corn cultivation that generates high volumes of corn stalks as a by-product. Because corn stalks are a rich source of carbon, they can be used as a raw material to prepare high-quantum-yield CQDs-NFP, while simultaneously reducing the environmental pollution caused by corn stalks. CQDs-NFP can be applied in several fields, including bioimaging (Kaur, & Verma, 2022), detection of heavy metals (Qu et al., 2022), pesticides (Huo et al., 2021), and veterinary drugs. The sensor response mechanisms can be classified as photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) (Wang et al., 2021).

In recent years, fluorescence sensors based on the IFE have been favored owing to their simple fabrication method and high sensitivity (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2020). The IFE occurs when other substances in a solution absorb at the excitation or emission wavelengths of the target molecule, thereby reducing the observed fluorescence. In solutions containing high concentrations of substances that absorb the excitation wavelength, the absorption of the incident light increases, which is equivalent to the intensity of the excitation light decreasing. Unlike other fluorescence response mechanisms, IFE does not involve any electronic and energy transfer processes, which simplifies the analysis (Wang et al., 2021). Furthermore, the analytical absorption signal is converted into fluorescence signal, which provides flexibility.

In this work, a CQDs-NFP was successfully prepared from corn stalks using an optimized one-step hydrothermal method, and a simple and highly sensitivity non-enzymatic biosensor was constructed for the rapid detection of MP. The linear range, sensitivity, and selectivity of the method was evaluated, and the detection mechanism was explored. Finally, the method was applied to the detection of MP in rice samples to demonstrate its feasibility and potential for widespread practical application. Compared with conventional large-scale instrument detection methods, this method is inexpensive, simple, and does not require skilled operators. In addition, it does not suffer from the changes in enzyme activity and limited detection conditions of enzyme-linked methods, and is not subject to the high cost and complex procedures required for the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers.

Corn stalks were collected in local farmers (Daqing City, Heilongjiang Province), and the p-nitrophenol, sodium chloride, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid were analytically pure and provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd.
Methyl parathion, fenthion, phoxim, trichlorfon, diazinon, and chlorpyrifos were purchased from Germany Dr. Ehrenstorfer Company.

200 high-speed pulverizer (Shanghai Yuan wo Industry and Trade Co., ltd.) and PHS.2C precision pH meter (American Mettler Toledo Company) were used. AB204-N analytical balance (Shanghai Mettler Toledo Instrument Co., ltd.) and rE-2000A vacuum rotatory evaporator (Ya Rong, Shanghai, China) were used.

Alpha1-2Ldplus Freeze Dryer (Germany Christ Company), KH-200 Hydrothermal Reactor (Gong Yi Ke Rui Instrument Co., ltd.), NICOLETiS10 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), TU-1810 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Beijing Pu Xi General Instrument Co., ltd.), RF-6000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and TEM-2100F Transmission Electron Microscope (Jeol ltd.) were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: After washing, drying and grinding, corn stalks powder was obtained through 80-mesh screens. A certain proportion of corn stalks powder with water was mixed. The mixture was transferred to a 200 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor to react for 10 h at 250 ℃. After the reaction, the solution was cooled to 25 ℃. The light-yellow liquid was obtained after vacuum filtration. The obtained light-yellow liquid was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. The filtrate was moved into a 1 kDa dialysis bag and dialysis for 2 days to remove small molecules. The CQDs-NFP solution was obtained. Finally, poured the solution into a container and placed in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe

ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of CQDs-NFP was measured by TEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy. Firstly, the CQDs-NFP powder was sonicated with ultra-thin carbon film. Finally, the particle size was observed with transmission electron microscopy.

FTIR spectrum of CQDs-NFP was measured by using NICOLETiS10 fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The sample (1 mg) and dried KBr (100 mg) were ground into a uniform powder in an agate mortar, and then the sample was put into a thin plate and scanned in the sample chamber. The wave-number scanning range is 4000–400 cm−1. The resolution was 2 cm−1. The characteristic peaks of the samples were recorded, and each sample was determined in triplicate.

The rice samples were purchased from farmer’s markets in Daqing city of Heilongjiang Province (China, December 2021) were ground into powder by using pulverizer and sifted through 80-mesh screens. The final specifications of spiked samples were 0.02, 0.04, 0.2, 0.4, 1, 2, 4, 8, 24, 40 and 56 µg MP added to the 5 g rice samples, respectively (all experiments are in triplicate). To ensure the MP to be well distributed, a reasonable amount of acetone was added to moisten the sample powders and careful agitation was performed followed by an air-drying at ambient temperature for 24 h before extraction. The spiked samples were kept in sealed bottles and stored.

The samples were extracted by QuEChERs method. Firstly, 5 g samples were mixed with 5 mL ultra-pure water and the mixture was vortexed for 1 min. Then 10 mL acetonitrile were added. The mixture with acetonitrile was vortexed for 1 min. Subsequently 5 g NaCl were added. The mixed solution was swirled for 1 min. After fully mixing, the mixture was dehydrated and salted out, and centrifuged at 2052 g for 10 min. The supernatant was vortexed for 1 min in a 50 mL centrifuge tube containing 450 mg of C18 and 1 g of NaCl and centrifuged at 10,611 g for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred to the chicken heart bottle, vacuumed until nearly dry, and finally redissolved with 200 µL methanol.

Under optimal conditions, the process of detecting MP using CQDs-NFP as follows. Firstly, 200 μL of CQDs-NFP solution and 3.6 mL of PBS buffer (0.2 mol/L, pH = 12) were put into a 5 mL centrifuge tube to form a uniformly distributed detection solution, and then 200 μL of solution containing different concentrations of MP were added to the detection solution. The mixture was incubated at 80 °C for 10 min and cooled to 25 ℃. The λex and λem are set to 360 and 440 nm, respectively. A standard curve was obtained by plotting the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentration of MP (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities without and with MP, respectively).

The important factors affecting hydrolysis of MP, including pH (5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, and 13), reaction temperature (30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 ℃), and reaction time (5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 35 min) were optimized in detail. For purpose of optimizing experiments and reducing errors, all experiments have to require in triplicate.

The stability was assessed by monitoring of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NFP. The CQDs-NFP was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃, and the fluorescence intensity was measured every 3 for 30 days. The change of fluorescence intensity was recorded to evaluate the stability of the detection method.

To evaluate the precision of CQDs-NFP sensor, the difference of fluorescence response between different batches and different parallel samples of the same batch during the detection process was studied under the optimal detection conditions to evaluate the precision of the constructed sensor in MP detection.

To evaluate the selectivity of this method, a series of pesticides with chemical structures similar to that of MP, namely fenthion, trichlorfon, chlorpyrifos, diazinon, and phoxim were analyzed. According to the above optimal detection conditions, the added concentration of each pesticide is 500 µg/mL, and the added concentration of MP is 40 µg/mL, incubated at 80 ℃ for 10 min to ensure complete reaction. Fluorescence intensity and absorbance were measured.
In order to explore the feasibility and practicability of the method, the stability and selectivity of the method were investigated by detecting actual rice samples.
Blank rice samples were extracted and fluorescence intensity was determined for 10 consecutive days. There were 10 groups of experiments, and each group was in triplicate.

A series of pesticides with chemical structures similar to that of MP, namely fenthion, trichlorfon, chlorpyrifos, diazinon and phoxiphos, were added into rice samples and then extracted. Using the optimal conditions, the added concentration of each pesticide was 500 µg/mL and the added concentration of MP was 40 µg/mL, and incubated at 80 ℃ for 10 min to ensure complete reaction. There were 6 groups of experiments, and each group of experiments was in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe

ce:section.ce:para: In Supplementary (Suppl.) Fig. 1 (A), the CQDs-NFP particles are spherical and well-dispersed in the aqueous solution. The CQDs-NFP particle dimensions were measured (Suppl. Fig. 1 (B)), and the particle size distribution was determined via statistical analysis of the particle sizes measured at 100 different points in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Suppl. Fig. 1 (A)). The particle size distribution indicates that the CQDs-NFP particles are 1.6–5.5 nm in size, with an average diameter of 3.1 nm, the majority of the particles are 2.0–3.0 nm in size. The measured particle sizes are similar to those of previously reported CQDs-NFP (Baslak, Demirel, Kocyigit, Alatli, & Yildirim, 2022; Kumari, & Sahu, 2022).

The spectrum in Suppl. Fig. 2 exhibits a prominent broad absorption peak at 3416 cm−1, which is attributed to the hydroxyl stretching vibration on the surface of the CQDs-NFP. The characteristic peak at 1611 cm−1 is attributed to the CC stretching vibration of the conjugate structure, the peak at 1384 cm−1 is due to the CH absorption, and the peaks at 1265 and 1115 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively. The FTIR spectra of the CQDs-NFP indicate, that the surface of the prepared CQDs-NFP contains functional groups such as hydroxyls and carboxyls, this structure is similar to those of other reported CQDs-NFP (Elsherbiny, Shao, Acheampong, Khalifa, Liu, & Huang, 2022; Castañeda-Serna, Calderón-Domínguez, García-Bórquez, Salgado-Cruz, & Farrera, 2022). The presence of these hydrophilic functional groups causes the prepared CQDs-NFP to be highly hydrophilic, which results in high water solubility.

The synthesis process of the CQDs-NFP and the detection mechanism of MP are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The detection mechanism was determined based on the fluorescence quenching of the CQDs-NFP. When MP is hydrolyzed, it produces p-nitrophenol (p-NP), which can quench the fluorescence of CQDs-NFP via the IFE (Chen et al., 2022), PET (Amiripour, Ghasemi, & Azizi, 2022), or FRET (Xu, Huang, Meng, & Yan, 2022) mechanisms. FRET-based sensing systems involve inter-molecular interactions between the chemical sensor and target molecule and require the sensor and target to be at a specific distance from each other or have complementary geometry. For the IFE, the absorption band of the absorbent overlaps to some extent with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore (Zhang et al., 2022). The IFE is a promising sensing technology that has been widely used in various chemical detection applications. The extensive fluorescence quenching caused by the strong absorption of the absorber provides an alternative platform for simple and sensitive detection. In this study, p-NP was used as the absorbent and MP was indirectly measured at a wavelength of 403 nm based on the IFE. The p-NP was produced using alkaline catalytic hydrolysis of MP, which resulted in a yellow solution.

The specific sensing mechanism between p-NP and the CQDs-NFP was examined. In Fig. 2 (A), the CQDs-NFP emits a strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm after excitation at 360 nm. In addition, the maximum absorption peak in the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum (blue curve) of p-NP is at 403 nm, which significantly overlaps with the excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of the CQDs-NFP, indicating the existence of FRET or IFE between the CQDs-NFP and p-NP.

To confirm that the interaction between p-NP and the CQDs-NFP occurs via the IFE and further understand the mechanism by which p-NP quench the CQDs-NFP fluorescence, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs-NFP solutions with (Fig. 2(B) (black curve) and without (red curve) p-NP were measured. The shape of the fluorescence attenuation curve does not change after the addition of p-NP to the CQDs-NFP solution. This indicates that there is no energy transfer between p-NP and the CQDs-NFP, which confirms that the interaction between p-NP and CQDs-NFP occurs through the IFE. This is consistent with published studies based on IFE theory (Zhang et al., 2022).

To examine the decomposition of MP to p-NP, the fluorescence intensity of MP was measured under acidic and alkaline conditions (Fig. 3 (A)). The fluorescence of the CQDs-NFP remained constant when MP was added to the reaction solution under acidic conditions, however, when MP was added to an alkaline solution (pH = 12), the fluorescence intensity of the solution decreased sharply, and the colour of the solution changed from colourless to yellow.

This indicates that MP rapidly hydrolyses to produce p-NP in a strong alkaline environment, and p-NP can quench the fluorescence of CQDs-NFP through the IFE. The degree of fluorescence quenching of the CQDs-NFP is related to the content of p-NP generated by the decomposition of MP under alkaline conditions. To determine whether MP can be completely hydrolyzed to p-NP, the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3(B)) of MP with different degrees of hydrolysis (black, red and blue spectra indicate undecomposed, partially decomposed and fully decomposed MP, respectively) were obtained. In the spectrum of unhydrolyzed MP, the absorption peak is at 300 nm, and after hydrolysis, the p-NP absorption peak appears at 403 nm. As the degree of hydrolysis increases, the absorption peak at 300 nm gradually weakens, and the absorption peak at 403 nm gradually increases, until the 300 nm absorption peak completely disappears, indicating that MP has been completely hydrolyzed. These results indicate that the IFE-based CQDs-NFP platform can be used for the quantitative analysis of MP.

The pH of the solution is the most critical factor in promoting the hydrolysis reaction, which affects the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-NFP and the hydrolysis rate of MP to p-NP. The optimum reaction pH was determined based on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence, respectively, of MP) of the CQDs-NFP, in the pH range of 5.0–13.0. In Suppl. Fig. 3 (A), MP is not hydrolyzed at pH < 7, and the fluorescence of CQDs-NFP is not quenched, because CQDs-NFP and MP are stable under acidic conditions. When the pH value is between 7 and 12, the fluorescence quenching efficiency gradually increases because the higher pH increases the efficiency of MP hydrolysis to p-NP. Consequently, the p-NP and CQDs-NFP interact via the IFE, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CQDs-NFP increases, reaching a maximum value at pH = 12. When the pH value is further increased, the fluorescence quenching efficiency does not continue to increase because MP is completely hydrolyzed to p-NP at pH = 12. According to these experimental results, pH = 12 is the optimal acid-base environment for the rapid conversion of MP to p-NP, this pH was used for the subsequent reaction time optimization experiment.

In general, temperature and reaction time primarily determine the reaction kinetics and the time to reach equilibrium. Therefore, the effects of temperature and time on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the sensor were further studied. The reaction temperature affected the efficiency and rate of the hydrolysis of MP to p-NP. The reaction temperature was set at 30–90 ℃ and the reaction mixture was incubated for a certain time. In Suppl. Fig. 3 (B), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CQDs-NFP and the fluorescence response of the sensor are positively correlated to the temperature in the range 30–80 ℃. This indicates that the higher number of molecular collisions due to the increase in kinetic energy at higher temperatures accelerates the hydrolysis rate of MP. Further increasing the temperature does not increase the hydrolysis rate of MP, nor does it increase the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CQDs-NFP, indicating that MP is completely hydrolyzed at 80 ℃. Based on these experimental results, 80 ℃ was selected as the optimal detection condition.

The effect of the reaction time on the hydrolysis rate of MP to p-NP was examined by setting the reaction time to 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 and 35 min. In Suppl. Fig. 3 (C), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CQDs-NFP first increases and then stable. The maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CQDs-NFP is reached after 10 min of heating. The fluorescence quenching efficiency remains stable at heating times greater than 10 min, indicating that the hydrolysis of MP to p-NP is complete after 10 min of heating. Therefore, the optimum reaction time was determined to be 10 min, this was used for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear range and limit of detection (LOD)
ce:section.ce:para: Using the optimal experimental conditions, we investigated the response of the CQDs-NFP towards MP. In Fig. 4 (A) and (B), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CQDs-NFP gradually increases with increasing MP concentration. The relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the MP concentration is linear in the range 0.005–14 µg/mL (F0/F = 0.1027X + 1.0323, R = 0.9994). The limit of detection (LOD) is 1.22 µg/kg (LOD = 3 × S/K, where S is the standard deviation. And K is the slope of the standard curve), and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) is 4.07 µg/kg (LOQ = 10 × S/K).

In addition, as the concentration of MP increases, the fluorescence of the CQDs-NFP is gradually quenched and the emission wavelength of the CQDs-NFP is gradually red shifted. There are two reasons for this phenomenon: (1) the added substance combines with functional groups on the surface of the CQDs-NFP, which alters the CQDs-NFP surface functional groups, and (2) the added substance reacts with the CQDs-NFP to produce a new substance. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs-NFP in the presence of various concentrations of MP were used to determine whether the CQDs-NFP fluorescence spectra were red shifted because the addition of MP produced new substances. In Suppl. Fig. 5, when various concentrations of MP were added to CQDs-NFP solutions, the characteristic absorption peak of p-NP appeared at 403 nm. As the MP concentration increases the peak position does not change, indicating that no new substances are produced. Therefore, the red shift of the CQDs-NFP fluorescence spectrum after the addition of MP, due to reactions between p-NP and the surface functional groups of the CQDs-NFP.

Matrix effects can be classified as matrix enhancement or suppression effects. In matrix enhancement the presence of matrix components enhances the detection signal, whereas in, matrix suppression, the presence of matrix components weakens the detection signal. In pesticide residue detection, the same pesticide can have different matrix effects in different types of plant-derived foods. In rice samples, MP is subject to a weak matrix suppression effect (Jia, Zhang, Zhang, He, Lin, & Ye, 2020).

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed sensor, a series of pesticides with chemical structures similar that of MP, namely phoxim, chlorpyrifos, diazinon, fenthion, and trichlorfon were analyzed using the optimal experimental conditions, and the fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorbance were determined. In the Suppl. Fig. 4 (A), (B), and (C), only MP has a clear fluorescence quenching effect on the CQDs-NFP, whereas the other pesticides have a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-NFP. In Suppl. Fig. 4 (B), the CQDs-NFP has a high selectivity in response to MP. When MP is added to the solution, and hydrolyzed to p-NP, a strong absorbance is observed at 403 nm, and the fluorescence of CQDs-NFP is simultaneously quenched. The interfering pesticides were added at a 10-fold excess concentration and could not be hydrolyzed to produce an absorbance at 403 nm. The fluorescence of CQDs-NFP is not quenched by interfering pesticides, therefore, this sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and specificity.

The stability of the sensor was evaluated through long-term monitoring of the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-NFP. The CQDs-NFP was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃, and the fluorescence intensity was measured every-three days. In Suppl. Fig. 5 (A), CQDs-NFP retained 91.18 % of the initially recorded fluorescence intensity after being stored for more than one month, indicating that the CQDs-NFP exhibited good stability when used as the signal output material of the fluorescence sensor.

To verify the precision of the CQDs-NFP sensor, the differences in the sensor fluorescence responses among different batches and different parallel samples of the same batch were studied under the optimal detection conditions. In Suppl. Fig. 5 (B), the maximum relative standard deviations (RSD) of the CQDs-NFP sensor for the detection of MP in 10 different batches and five parallel samples of the same batch are 2.89 and 2.52 %, respectively, indicating that the developed CQDs-NFP fluorescence sensor exhibits high precision.

In Suppl. Fig. 6, the maximum RSD was 1.23 % after 10 consecutive days of testing actual samples under the optimal testing conditions. A series of pesticides with chemical structures similar to that of MP, namely fenthion, trichlorfon, chlorpyrifos, diazinon and phoxim were added to rice samples. MP had a significant fluorescence quenching effect on the CQDs-NFP, whereas the other pesticides had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-NFP. In conclusion, this method exhibits has high stability and selectivity for the detection of MP in rice samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MP in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of the sensor in an actual sample, rice samples were analyzed using the sensor system established in this study. The recoveries of the standard addition range from 91.64 to 104.28 % (Table 1 ) and the RSD is less than 4.17 %, which indicates that the established detection sensor system has high accuracy and stability when used for the analysis of actual samples. MP was not detected in the unspiked rice samples.

The results from the sensor developed in this study were compared to those of other reported fluorescence methods (Zhang et al., 2019; Mehta, Dhaka, Paul, Dayananda, & Deep, 2019; Yi, Zeng, & Zhu, 2021; Li, Wan, Yan, Qiu, & Wang, 2020; Li, Chen, Lin, Chen, & Qiu, 2020; Liu, Peng, Wu, He, & Qiu, 2021). Table 2 , lists, the methods, probes, LODs, detection times and other relevant parameters of each study. The proposed method shows relatively high sensitivity. The detection process proceeds via catalytic alkaline hydrolysis without the addition of any other reagents or enzymes. This method has the advantages of environmental friendliness, cost effectiveness, simplicity, good stability, high selectivity, and easily controlled detection conditions. It provides a new strategy for the rapid detection of MP residues in rice.

In summary, this thesis successfully constructed a highly sensitive, environmental friendliness, inexpensive, good stability, and excellent selectivity non-enzymatic fluorescent sensor for rapid and efficient detection of MP in rice samples. Using corn stalks as raw material to prepare CQDs-NFP not only opens up a new way to use corn stalks, but also realizes the green recycling of biological waste and protects the environment. The size distribution of the prepared CQDs-NFP is uniform, good dispersion. From raw materials to synthetic methods, we all adopt a green and environmentally friendly way, which is in line with the concept of protecting the ecological environment. The detection principle is that p-NP is hydrolyzed by MP under alkaline conditions, and fluorescence quenching occurs between p-NP and CQDs-NFP due to inner filter effect. The method has high sensitivity and stability. The method has been successfully applied to the rapid detection of MP in actual samples, with the recoveries of 91.64–104.28 % and RSD were less than 4.17 %. The results of this study show that the method is effective and feasible for the detection of complex rice samples.
Ruiting Zhang: Writing – original draft. Liyuan Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Runzhong Yu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Changyuan Wang: Resources, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Government for the Reform and Development of Local Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant number: 2020YQ16), the key research and development program guidance project of Heilongjiang province (Grant number: GZ20210071), the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant number: 2018YFE0206300).
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135679.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
